title: Editorial: Redefining ‘the Artist’ through New Narratives
content:
Dr Anna-Maria von Bonsdorff, Director, Ateneum Art Museum / Finnish National Gallery

25 March 2026

Finnish art is now more visible and present internationally than ever. Museums, such as New York’s Metropolitan Museum of Art, are acquiring Finnish art and Finnish art is present in many exhibitions in major museums around Europe and the US. This interest is growing. However, it also means that, as museum professionals and curators at the Finnish National Gallery, we are facing the fact that it is even more important that knowledge of Finnish history is expertly disseminated to our counterparts abroad, in addition to explaining the context the artists were and are working in. It also means a shift away from the emphasis on the solitary genius artist.

In this issue of FNG Research, there is a significant focus on ‘the artist’ and in several articles we highlight the many well-known names in Finnish art history, as well as new research and fresh approaches to these artists’ narratives. sourceDescription: FNG Research is the online magazine of the Finnish National Gallery. This online publication aims to promote the research activities of the Gallery, research being a part of its core functions. The Finnish National Gallery consists of three museums – the Ateneum Art Museum, the Museum of Contemporary Art Kiasma, the Sinebrychoff Art Museum – and the Department for Collections Management. FNG Research is a magazine for art-museum and art-history professionals internationally. The site is updated bi-monthly with new developments and content.

title: Conservation of Four Sketches, by Akseli Gallen-Kallela, for the Frescoes at the Finnish Pavilion at the 1900 Paris World’s Fair
content:
Ilona Osara BA, Paper Conservator, Finnish National Gallery

—

In 1898, three Finnish architects, Eliel Saarinen, Armas Lindgren and Herman Gesellius won the competition to design Finland’s pavilion at the 1900 Paris World’s Fair. Since 1896, Akseli Gallen-Kallela (1865–1931) had been creating paintings inspired by the Kalevala, the Finnish national epic, each work contributing to the emerging Finnish national identity. He was commissioned to paint murals for the Finnish Pavilion’s Iris Room and to decorate the arches of the pavilion, giving him an opportunity to present his art to an international audience.[1]

The Finnish Pavilion at the 1900 Paris World’s Fair became a landmark project that showcased Finland as a modern and distinct nation. Featuring the finest Finnish art, design, and industry, it earned widespread acclaim.[2] Gallen-Kallela himself was awarded a gold medal by the World’s Fair for his contributions.[3]
